topic: European digital speech and political content moderation (last 7 days)

articles.headline_en: French government under fire after Emmanuel Macron’s campaign video is deleted from X due to suspected AI use
articles.summary_en: Le Monde reports that a campaign-style video posted by Emmanuel Macron’s entourage on X was removed by the platform after being flagged under its rules on synthetic or manipulated media.[1] The clip, promoting Macron’s party ahead of political events, allegedly relied on AI‑altered visuals, triggering X’s automated moderation and subsequent takedown.[1] Macron allies have criticised the removal as opaque and politically sensitive in the middle of an electoral period.[1]
articles.significance: This case highlights how platform AI‑content rules can directly affect sitting heads of state during campaigns, raising transparency and neutrality concerns about private platforms’ power over political speech in Europe.

articles.headline_en: German AfD politician deletes X post after outrage over offensive comment about migrants
articles.summary_en: According to Der Spiegel, a regional Alternative für Deutschland (AfD) politician deleted a post on X following heavy criticism for comments widely described as racist toward migrants.[2] The post had circulated broadly before deletion, prompting condemnation from other German parties and civil‑society groups, and calls for party disciplinary measures.[2] Screenshots of the deleted post continue to circulate online, keeping the controversy alive despite the takedown.[2]
articles.significance: The incident illustrates how deletion of controversial posts by populist politicians rarely ends a scandal and underlines the continuing struggle in Germany over extremist speech and accountability on social platforms.

articles.headline_en: UK government faces backlash after minister deletes post attacking judges over online safety ruling
articles.summary_en: The Guardian reports that a UK cabinet minister deleted a social media post that accused senior judges of undermining public safety following a court decision related to online safety and platform liability.[3] Legal experts and opposition MPs warned that the original post risked eroding trust in the judiciary and breaching norms on political attacks against the courts, prompting its removal.[3] The government declined to say whether the deletion followed internal pressure from No 10 or the Cabinet Office.[3]
articles.significance: The episode underscores tension between the executive and judiciary over the regulation of online platforms, and shows how political messaging about digital policy can cross into attacks on rule‑of‑law institutions.

articles.headline_en: Italian data protection authority orders removal of political micro‑targeting ads on Meta during campaign period
articles.summary_en: La Repubblica reports that Italy’s Garante per la protezione dei dati personali ordered Meta to suspend and remove certain political micro‑targeted ads on Facebook and Instagram during an active electoral campaign.[4] The authority found that the company’s ad‑targeting practices for campaign messages did not comply with EU GDPR transparency and consent requirements, and demanded immediate removal of non‑compliant content.[4] Meta has said it is reviewing the order and adjusting its tools for Italian users.[4]
articles.significance: This enforcement action shows European data‑protection law being used as a lever to restrict and reshape how political speech is distributed and targeted on major platforms.

articles.headline_en: Spain’s Supreme Court orders regional government to reinstate deleted pro‑independence comments from official Facebook page
articles.summary_en: El País reports that Spain’s Supreme Court ruled that a regional administration unlawfully removed comments supporting Catalan independence from its official Facebook page.[5] The court held that the moderation policy discriminated based on political opinion and violated citizens’ constitutional rights to freedom of expression and participation in public affairs.[5] Authorities were ordered both to restore the comments and to revise their moderation rules.[5]
articles.significance: The judgment clarifies that European public authorities running official social media must respect viewpoint neutrality, strengthening legal protections for political speech in state‑run digital spaces.

articles.headline_en: Polish government seeks court order forcing X to remove accounts spreading election‑related disinformation
articles.summary_en: Gazeta Wyborcza reports that Poland’s Ministry of Digital Affairs has petitioned a Warsaw court to compel X to remove several accounts accused of systematically spreading false information about the integrity of upcoming elections.[6] Officials argue that the activity amounts to coordinated disinformation that threatens national security and democratic processes, justifying judicial intervention.[6] Digital‑rights groups warn that the move, if broad or vague, could open the door to politically motivated censorship of online critics.[6]
articles.significance: The case illustrates the growing use of court mechanisms in Central Europe to pressure platforms over political content, raising difficult questions about distinguishing disinformation from protected political speech.

articles.headline_en: Irish data regulator questions TikTok over removal of videos criticising EU migration pact
articles.summary_en: The Irish Times reports that Ireland’s Data Protection Commission has asked TikTok to explain the removal or down‑ranking of several videos in which users criticised the EU’s migration and asylum pact.[7] Activists claim that the videos did not contain hate speech or misinformation and argue that they were targeted for their political stance; TikTok says removals were due to violations of community‑guideline rules on harassment.[7] The regulator is examining whether any profiling or automated moderation practices unlawfully affected users’ political expression.[7]
articles.significance: This inquiry highlights regulatory scrutiny of opaque platform moderation around contentious EU policy debates and could influence how TikTok treats political commentary in Europe.

articles.headline_en: Swedish MP apologises and deletes Instagram story calling climate activists “eco‑terrorists”
articles.summary_en: Dagens Nyheter reports that a Swedish member of parliament deleted an Instagram story after describing climate activists as “eco‑terrorists” following a disruptive protest.[8] Opposition politicians and NGOs accused the MP of stoking hostility and trivialising terrorism, leading to a public apology and removal of the content.[8] The MP’s party leadership is reported to have privately warned members about inflammatory rhetoric on social media.[8]
articles.significance: The episode reflects broader Nordic debates on political tone online and demonstrates how elected officials are being pushed to moderate their language about protest movements on social media.

articles.headline_en: Dutch court orders Telegram to remove channels doxxing and harassing local politicians
articles.summary_en: NRC reports that a Dutch district court instructed Telegram to block several channels that had been publishing personal details and organising harassment campaigns against local councillors and mayors.[9] The lawsuit was brought by a group of targeted politicians with support from a civic‑rights organisation, arguing that the channels posed a serious threat to safety and democratic participation.[9] Telegram has begun geo‑blocking the channels in the Netherlands while it considers further legal options.[9]
articles.significance: The ruling confirms that European courts are willing to order strong measures against online spaces that facilitate intimidation of politicians, even on encrypted or semi‑anonymous platforms.

articles.headline_en: European Commission questions X over handling of far‑right politicians’ content under the Digital Services Act
articles.summary_en: Politico Europe reports that the European Commission has sent a new request for information to X under the Digital Services Act, focusing on how the platform moderates content from far‑right political actors in several EU countries.[10] Brussels is seeking detailed data on takedown rates, appeals, and recommendation‑algorithm treatment of posts that may qualify as hate speech or disinformation during the current electoral cycle.[10] X must respond within a set deadline, and failure to provide satisfactory answers could lead to formal proceedings and potential fines.[10]
articles.significance: The move underscores the EU’s increasing readiness to use DSA transparency and enforcement tools to scrutinise platform decisions that directly affect the visibility of controversial politicians’ speech.

editorial_overview: Across Europe this week, digital speech conflicts are concentrated at the intersection of election politics, platform rules, and state regulation. Several stories involve politicians posting inflammatory or controversial content and then deleting it under pressure, which rarely ends the controversy as screenshots continue to circulate. Courts and regulators in Spain, Italy, the Netherlands, Ireland, and Poland are actively shaping the boundaries of online political speech, oscillating between protecting expression and combating threats such as harassment or disinformation. At the EU level, the Commission’s use of the Digital Services Act against major platforms signals a shift from informal pressure to structured legal oversight of how political content is moderated. Overall, the region is moving toward a more legalistic and contested framework for governing political expression on commercial and official digital platforms.
